scenarios.reason: A 5.5 Msun MS star would contribute ~5.1% of primary flux. This is detectable; no secondary SED is seen.
f(M) = 1.51 Msun exceeds the Chandrasekhar limit (1.44 Msun) by a factor of 1.05. No WD can have this mass.

f(M) = 1.51 Msun is below the NS ceiling (2.3 Msun), so a NS is NOT excluded by the mass function alone. However, at fiducial M1 = 5.0 Msun, M2_min = 5.5 Msun exceeds the NS ceiling. The NS scenario depends on the primary mass estimate.

Stability requires P_inner < 130.6 d. Two ~2.8 Msun MS stars would each have L ~ 73 Lsun. If both are compact objects, the inner binary would need M_total > 5.5 Msun — still requiring at least one BH-mass object. Near-zero eccentricity (e=0.006) also disfavours a triple system where Kozai-Lidov oscillations would pump eccentricity.
A 5.5 Msun stripped star would have Teff > 30000 K with strong UV excess and He II emission. No UV data available. Cannot be fully excluded without UV spectroscopy.

The NSS significance sigma = 343.4 is very high (68x the catalogue threshold of 5). The orbital elements (P=620.0d, e=0.006) are physically consistent. However, RUWE = 2.32 exceeds the 1.4 threshold, indicating astrometric residuals beyond a single-star model. The elevated RUWE may arise from the binary orbital motion itself or from the close neighbor at 3.3 arcsec. Independent RV confirmation is essential to exclude systematics.

key_argument: f(M) = 1.51 Msun exceeds the Chandrasekhar limit but falls below the NS ceiling (2.3 Msun). The BH case depends on the primary mass: at fiducial M1 = 5.0 Msun, M2_min = 5.5 Msun enters BH territory. The very high NSS significance (sigma = 343.4) and physically coherent orbital elements support a genuine binary.
